title: Analyzing Credit Union Bank Statements: What's Different and What to Watch For
meta: Credit union statements have unique formatting compared to big banks. Learn how to analyze statements from credit unions for income verification and risk assessment.
content:
Credit Unions vs. Big Banks: Statement Differences

Approximately 130 million Americans are credit union members, making credit union bank statements a common document in lending. While the core information is the same, credit union statements often differ from big bank statements in formatting, terminology, and organization.

Common Credit Union Terminology

Credit unions use different terms for some familiar concepts:

  • Share account = Savings account
  • Share draft account = Checking account
  • Share certificate = Certificate of deposit (CD)
  • Dividends = Interest paid on deposits (credit unions are member-owned)

Formatting Differences

Credit union statements typically show:

  • All transactions in a single chronological list rather than separated into deposits/withdrawals sections
  • Running balance column showing balance after each transaction
  • More descriptive transaction labels in many cases
  • Different fee structures (many credit unions charge lower or no fees)

Income Verification on Credit Union Statements

Direct deposits at credit unions typically appear as 'ACH Credit' with the employer name. The chronological format requires analysts to scan through all transactions to identify income deposits rather than going directly to a deposits section.

Lower Fees Don't Mean No Risk

Credit unions often charge lower fees and have more lenient overdraft policies. A credit union account might show fewer NSF fees than a big bank account with similar financial behavior. Don't let lower fee totals mask underlying cash flow problems — look at balance patterns and overdraft events regardless of fee amounts.

Automated Credit Union Statement Analysis

StatementScrub handles all major credit union statement formats including Navy Federal, USAA, PenFed, State Employees, and local credit unions, regardless of their specific formatting differences.
